title: Docker Container Networking Basics
text: Docker containers communicate with each other using virtual networks. By default, Docker creates a bridge network when you install it. Containers connected to the same bridge network can communicate using IP addresses. To allow containers to communicate by name instead of IP, you should create a user-defined bridge network using 'docker network create'. Containers on a user-defined network can resolve each other's names automatically through Docker's built-in DNS server. This is different from the default bridge network, which does not support automatic DNS resolution between containers. If you need containers to talk to services outside the Docker host, you typically use port mapping with the -p flag, which forwards a port on the host machine to a port inside the container.

title: PostgreSQL Connection Pooling
text: Connection pooling in PostgreSQL helps manage database connections efficiently, especially under high load. Without pooling, every new client connection requires PostgreSQL to fork a new backend process, which is expensive in terms of memory and CPU. Tools like PgBouncer sit between your application and PostgreSQL, maintaining a pool of reusable connections. PgBouncer supports three pooling modes: session pooling, transaction pooling, and statement pooling. Transaction pooling is the most commonly used mode in production because it releases the connection back to the pool as soon as a transaction completes, allowing many clients to share a small number of actual database connections. This significantly reduces the overhead of connection establishment for applications with many short-lived database interactions.

title: FastAPI Dependency Injection
text: FastAPI's dependency injection system allows you to declare shared logic that multiple path operations can reuse. You define a dependency as a function, and FastAPI automatically calls it before executing your endpoint. Dependencies can themselves depend on other dependencies, forming a chain that FastAPI resolves automatically. This pattern is commonly used for authentication, database session management, and shared validation logic. Unlike some other frameworks, FastAPI dependencies are resolved per-request, meaning each incoming request gets a fresh call to the dependency function unless you explicitly cache the result. You can override dependencies during testing, which makes it easy to substitute a real database connection with a mock one without changing your endpoint code.

title: Understanding Gradient Descent
text: Gradient descent is an optimization algorithm used to minimize a loss function by iteratively moving in the direction of steepest descent. At each step, the algorithm computes the gradient of the loss function with respect to the model parameters, then updates the parameters by subtracting a fraction of this gradient. The fraction is controlled by the learning rate. If the learning rate is too large, the algorithm may overshoot the minimum and fail to converge. If it is too small, training will be very slow. Variants like stochastic gradient descent compute the gradient using a single training example or small batch, rather than the entire dataset, which makes training feasible on large datasets. Momentum-based methods add a fraction of the previous update to the current one, which helps accelerate convergence and reduce oscillation.

title: FAISS Index Types Explained
text: FAISS provides several index types for different tradeoffs between search speed, memory usage, and accuracy. The simplest index, IndexFlatL2, performs an exhaustive search by comparing the query vector against every vector in the index, guaranteeing exact results but scaling poorly with dataset size. IndexIVFFlat partitions the vector space into clusters using k-means, and at search time only examines vectors within the closest clusters, trading some accuracy for significant speed improvements. For very large datasets, IndexIVFPQ additionally compresses vectors using product quantization, drastically reducing memory usage at the cost of some precision. Choosing the right index depends on your dataset size, latency requirements, and available memory. For datasets under a million vectors, a flat index is often fast enough and avoids the complexity of tuning cluster parameters.

title: MLflow Experiment Tracking
text: MLflow Tracking allows you to log parameters, metrics, and artifacts from your machine learning experiments so you can compare runs later. Each run is recorded under an experiment, and you can log values using simple function calls like mlflow.log_param and mlflow.log_metric within your training script. MLflow automatically records the start and end time of each run, along with the source code version if you are using git. The MLflow UI provides a way to visually compare metrics across multiple runs, which is useful when tuning hyperparameters. Artifacts such as trained models, plots, and configuration files can also be logged and later downloaded from the tracking server. MLflow also supports a model registry, which helps manage the lifecycle of models from staging to production.

title: REST API Rate Limiting Strategies
text: Rate limiting protects an API from being overwhelmed by too many requests in a short period. The token bucket algorithm is one of the most widely used approaches: each client is allocated a bucket that refills with tokens at a fixed rate, and each request consumes one token. If the bucket is empty, the request is rejected or queued. The sliding window algorithm is another common approach that tracks request timestamps within a rolling time window, offering more precise control than fixed window counting. Rate limits can be applied per API key, per IP address, or per user account, depending on the desired granularity. Well-designed APIs typically return rate limit information in response headers, such as the number of remaining requests and when the limit resets, so clients can adjust their behavior proactively.

title: Sentence Transformers for Semantic Search
text: Sentence Transformers are models specifically fine-tuned to produce sentence embeddings where semantically similar sentences are close together in vector space. Unlike raw BERT embeddings, which are not well-suited for direct comparison using cosine similarity, Sentence Transformers use a siamese network structure during training to optimize embeddings for this exact use case. Popular models like all-MiniLM-L6-v2 offer a good balance between embedding quality and inference speed, making them suitable for real-time semantic search applications. When building a semantic search system, it's important to use the same model for encoding both the documents and the queries, since embeddings from different models are not comparable. Normalizing embeddings to unit length before computing cosine similarity is also a common practice that can simplify similarity calculations to a simple dot product.

title: Kubernetes Pod Scheduling
text: The Kubernetes scheduler is responsible for assigning newly created pods to nodes in the cluster. Scheduling decisions are based on resource requirements, hardware constraints, affinity and anti-affinity rules, and taints and tolerations. Node affinity allows you to constrain which nodes a pod can be scheduled on based on node labels, while pod affinity and anti-affinity control scheduling based on the presence of other pods. Taints are applied to nodes to repel pods that do not have a matching toleration, which is commonly used to reserve nodes for specific workloads. If the scheduler cannot find a suitable node for a pod, the pod remains in a Pending state until a suitable node becomes available or the constraints are relaxed. Resource requests and limits also influence scheduling, since the scheduler avoids placing pods on nodes that lack sufficient available resources.

title: Handling Class Imbalance in Machine Learning
text: Class imbalance occurs when one class significantly outnumbers another in a classification dataset, which can cause models to become biased toward predicting the majority class. Common techniques to address this include oversampling the minority class, undersampling the majority class, or generating synthetic samples using methods like SMOTE. Adjusting the class weights in the loss function is another effective approach, where misclassifying the minority class is penalized more heavily during training. Evaluation metrics also need careful consideration in imbalanced settings, since accuracy can be misleading; metrics like precision, recall, F1 score, and ROC-AUC provide a more complete picture of model performance. Stratified sampling should be used when splitting data into train and test sets to ensure both sets maintain the same class distribution as the original dataset.

title: Git Rebase vs Merge
text: Git offers two primary strategies for integrating changes from one branch into another: merge and rebase. A merge creates a new commit that combines the histories of both branches, preserving the exact history of when and how changes were made, including any parallel work. A rebase, on the other hand, moves the entire branch to begin on the tip of another branch, effectively rewriting commit history to create a linear sequence of commits. Rebasing produces a cleaner, more readable project history but should be avoided on commits that have already been pushed to a shared branch, since it changes commit hashes and can cause conflicts for collaborators. Interactive rebase allows you to edit, squash, or reorder commits before they are applied, which is useful for cleaning up a messy commit history before merging a feature branch.

title: Caching Strategies with Redis
text: Redis is commonly used as a caching layer to reduce load on primary databases and improve application response times. The cache-aside pattern is one of the most common strategies: the application first checks Redis for the requested data, and if it is not found, the application queries the database and stores the result in Redis for future requests. Time-to-live values are typically set on cached entries to prevent stale data from persisting indefinitely. Write-through caching, in contrast, updates the cache at the same time as the database, ensuring consistency but adding latency to write operations. Cache invalidation remains one of the harder problems in caching, since determining exactly when cached data becomes stale often requires careful design around how and when underlying data changes.

title: Caching Strategies with Memcached
text: Memcached is a simple, high-performance, distributed memory caching system originally designed to speed up dynamic web applications by reducing database load. Unlike Redis, Memcached stores data purely in memory with no persistence to disk, and it does not support complex data structures like sorted sets or hashes, only simple key-value pairs. Memcached distributes data across multiple servers using client-side consistent hashing, meaning the client library decides which server a given key belongs to. Because Memcached is multithreaded by design, it can take better advantage of multi-core machines for a single cache instance compared to Redis, which is largely single-threaded for command execution. Memcached is often chosen over Redis specifically when the use case is pure caching with no need for persistence, pub/sub, or advanced data types.

title: MySQL Connection Pooling
text: Connection pooling in MySQL reduces the overhead of repeatedly opening and closing database connections, which can be expensive due to TCP handshakes and authentication. MySQL itself does not include a built-in connection pooler in the traditional sense; instead, pooling is typically handled at the application layer using libraries like mysql2's pool feature in Node.js, or HikariCP in Java applications. HikariCP is widely regarded as one of the fastest connection pool implementations available, using a lightweight design that avoids unnecessary locking. A common misconfiguration is setting the pool size too high, which can overwhelm the MySQL server with more concurrent connections than it can efficiently handle, since MySQL's max_connections setting caps how many simultaneous connections the server will accept regardless of pool size on the client.

title: Docker Compose for Multi-Container Applications
text: Docker Compose allows you to define and run multi-container Docker applications using a single YAML configuration file, typically named docker-compose.yml. Each service defined in the file corresponds to a container, and Compose automatically creates a shared network so that services can communicate with each other using the service name as a hostname, similar to how a user-defined bridge network works with plain Docker. Compose is primarily intended for local development and testing environments rather than production orchestration, where tools like Kubernetes are more commonly used. The 'depends_on' field controls the startup order of services, but it does not wait for a service to be fully ready, only for the container to have started, which is a common source of race conditions when one service needs another to be fully initialized first.

title: The Adam Optimizer
text: Adam is an optimization algorithm that combines ideas from momentum and adaptive learning rates to train neural networks more efficiently than plain gradient descent. It maintains two moving averages for each parameter: the first moment, which is an exponentially decaying average of past gradients, and the second moment, which is an exponentially decaying average of past squared gradients. These two estimates allow Adam to adapt the effective learning rate for each parameter individually, giving larger updates to parameters with small or infrequent gradients and smaller updates to parameters with large or frequent gradients. Adam also includes a bias-correction step early in training to counteract the fact that the moving averages are initialized at zero and are therefore biased toward zero during the first few updates. Despite its popularity, Adam can sometimes generalize worse than plain stochastic gradient descent with momentum on certain tasks, which is why some practitioners still prefer SGD for final fine-tuning stages.

title: HNSW for Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search
text: HNSW, or Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs, is an algorithm for approximate nearest neighbor search that builds a multi-layer graph structure to enable fast search in high-dimensional vector spaces. Unlike FAISS's IVF-based indices, which rely on clustering the vector space into partitions, HNSW organizes vectors into a graph where each node is connected to its approximate nearest neighbors, and search proceeds by greedily traversing the graph from a random entry point in the top layer down to the bottom layer. HNSW generally achieves excellent recall and low latency without requiring a training or clustering step beforehand, unlike IVF-based FAISS indices which need to be trained on representative data first. The tradeoff is memory usage, since HNSW graphs typically require more memory per vector than compressed FAISS indices like IVFPQ. Libraries like hnswlib and Qdrant use HNSW as their core search algorithm.

title: Kubernetes Service Networking
text: A Kubernetes Service provides a stable network endpoint for a set of pods, since individual pod IP addresses are ephemeral and change whenever a pod is recreated. When a Service is created, Kubernetes assigns it a stable virtual IP address called a ClusterIP, and kube-proxy on each node configures networking rules so that traffic sent to that ClusterIP gets load-balanced across the pods matching the Service's label selector. This is different from Docker's default networking model, where containers typically communicate directly using container names resolved through an embedded DNS server, without any load-balancing layer in between. For exposing a Service outside the cluster, Kubernetes offers NodePort, which opens a specific port on every node, and LoadBalancer, which provisions an external load balancer from the cloud provider. Ingress resources provide a more flexible way to route external HTTP traffic to multiple Services based on hostname or path, without needing a separate LoadBalancer for each Service.

title: Distributed Tracing in Microservices
text: As applications grow into dozens or hundreds of microservices, understanding why a single user request is slow becomes difficult because that request may pass through many services before completing. Distributed tracing solves this by assigning a unique trace ID to each incoming request, which gets propagated through HTTP headers to every downstream service the request touches. Each service records a span representing the work it performed, including a start time, duration, and any relevant metadata, and all spans sharing the same trace ID are later stitched together into a single timeline. This makes it possible to see exactly which service in a request's path introduced the most latency. One subtlety that often causes incomplete traces in production is that asynchronous processing, such as messages placed on a queue for later processing, requires manually propagating the trace context, since the automatic instrumentation that works for synchronous HTTP calls does not automatically follow messages through a queue. Popular tools in this space include Jaeger, Zipkin, and OpenTelemetry, with OpenTelemetry increasingly becoming the vendor-neutral standard for instrumentation across languages. Sampling is another important consideration: tracing every single request at high scale can be prohibitively expensive to store, so most production systems only trace a percentage of requests, though critical operations like checkout flows are often force-sampled at 100% regardless of the general sampling rate to ensure they are always debuggable.

title: Database Indexing with B-Trees
text: Most relational databases, including PostgreSQL and MySQL's InnoDB engine, use B-Tree indexes as the default index type because they perform well for both equality lookups and range queries. A B-Tree keeps its keys sorted and maintains a balanced structure where every leaf node is at the same depth, which guarantees that lookups, insertions, and deletions all complete in logarithmic time relative to the number of rows. When a query filters on a column that has a B-Tree index, the database can quickly narrow down the search instead of scanning every row in the table, an operation known as a sequential scan. However, indexes are not free: every insert or update on an indexed column requires the database to also update the index structure, which adds write overhead, and indexes consume additional disk space. A common mistake is over-indexing a table, adding indexes on columns that are rarely queried, which slows down write-heavy workloads without providing meaningful read performance benefits. Composite indexes, which span multiple columns, are most effective when the query's filter conditions match the same column order the index was created with.
